did_this_paper_use_ai_to_obtain_results_.details: The paper developed and evaluated Saliency-Enhanced Content-Based Image Retrieval (SE-CBIR), which utilizes Convolutional Neural Networks (CNNs) (specifically EfficientNetB4) for feature extraction and saliency maps for interpretability in dermatology. The study used the HAM10000 dataset of dermoscopic images to train the model and conducted a reader study to assess its impact on diagnosis accuracy and confidence for skin lesions (melanoma, basal cell carcinoma, etc.).

is_this_paper_a_review_paper_or_a_resear.details: The paper is explicitly labeled as an 'Original Paper' and describes a study that developed a new image retrieval architecture (SE-CBIR), conducted a reader study at the University Hospital Zurich Dermatology Clinic, and reported original experimental results including retrieval accuracy improvements (7%) and diagnosis accuracy increases (22%).

title: Saliency-Enhanced Content-Based Image Retrieval for Diagnosis Support in Dermatology Consultation: Reader Study
brief_summary: This study developed Saliency-Enhanced Content-Based Image Retrieval (SE-CBIR), a deep learning architecture using EfficientNetB4 and saliency maps to improve interpretability and feature extraction for skin lesion diagnosis. The model was trained and evaluated on the HAM10000 dataset of dermoscopic images. A reader study involving dermatologists and residents assessed the tool's impact on retrieval accuracy and diagnostic performance. The results showed that SE-CBIR improved retrieval accuracy by 7% compared to traditional CBIR and increased diagnosis accuracy by 22% when used as a support tool. Additionally, the tool increased diagnostic confidence by 24% and reduced melanoma overdiagnosis by 53%.

lesion_types_studied.lesion_type: Actinic keratosis, squamous cell carcinoma, and Bowen disease

all_task_types.target: Dermoscopic images similar to a query image
all_task_types.details: The primary AI task is Content-Based Image Retrieval (CBIR), specifically Saliency-Enhanced CBIR (SE-CBIR). The model extracts features from dermoscopic images using a CNN (EfficientNetB4) and retrieves the most similar images from a labeled dataset (HAM10000) to assist dermatologists in diagnosis. The system ranks images based on feature similarity.

all_task_types.target: Lesion class labels (e.g., melanoma, nevus, basal cell carcinoma)
all_task_types.details: The underlying CNN backbone was trained as a multiclass classifier for 7 pigmented lesion categories using the HAM10000 dataset. However, the primary contribution and evaluation metric of the study focus on the retrieval performance and its impact on human diagnostic accuracy, rather than the AI model directly outputting a classification label as the final result for the user.

datasets_used.name: HAM10000
datasets_used.description: A public dataset of 10,015 labeled dermoscopic images of pigmented skin lesions. The ground truth was determined by histopathology in more than 50% of images, with the remainder confirmed by follow-up examinations, expert consensus, or in vivo confocal microscopy. Follow-up images were removed to maximize diversity.
classes_used: actinic keratosis, squamous cell carcinoma, and Bowen disease (akiec)
benign keratosis (bkl; solar lentigo, seborrheic keratosis, and lichen planus-like keratosis)
vascular lesions (vasc; angiomas, angiokeratomas, pyogenic granulomas, and hemorrhage)

use.training: Used to train the EfficientNetB4 classifier backbone and for the fine-tuning stage with saliency maps.
use.testing: Used as the source for the test set from which 100 randomly chosen dermoscopic images were extracted for the reader study (Task 1 and Task 2). Also used as the retrieval database for evaluating retrieval accuracy.

statistical_methods_used.purpose: To assess inter-rater agreement and consistency of participant selections between Task 1 and Task 2.
To measure the spread or variability of diagnosis accuracy performance among participants.
To compute the diagnosis accuracy for the SE-CBIR algorithm by aggregating predictions from the k=6 best-performing participants.

statistical_methods_used.method: Per-class prediction precision (P@k) and Average precision (AP@k)
statistical_methods_used.purpose: To evaluate and compare the retrieval accuracy of the traditional CBIR versus the SE-CBIR algorithms for different numbers of retrieved images (k=1, 3, 6, 9).

hypothesis_testing.interpretation: The paper did not report formal hypothesis testing (e.g., p-values or significance tests) for the comparison of diagnosis accuracy or confidence. It reported descriptive statistics (percentages, means, standard deviations) and Cohen's kappa coefficients for inter-rater agreement, but did not perform inferential statistical tests to determine statistical significance of the improvements.

HAM10000 (Retrieval Evaluation).metric: Per-class prediction precision (P@k) and Average precision (AP@k)
HAM10000 (Retrieval Evaluation).notes: SE-CBIR improved retrieval accuracy by 7% (77% vs 84%) for single-lesion retrieval (k=1) compared to traditional CBIR. The improvement in AP@k increased with k, from 7% (k=1) to 18% (k=9). The largest per-class difference was found for class df. Class akiec did not experience significant improvement due to underrepresentation (2% of retrieval dataset) and limited samples.

HAM10000 (Reader Study - Task 1: Baselin.notes: Task 1 involved 9 volunteers (1 board-certified dermatologist, 8 residents) diagnosing 100 randomly chosen dermoscopic images without AI support. The lowest accuracy was recorded at 38% by one participant. Inter-rater agreement was fair.

HAM10000 (Reader Study - Task 2: SE-CBIR.metric: Diagnosis Accuracy, Confidence, and Overdiagnosis Reduction
HAM10000 (Reader Study - Task 2: SE-CBIR.notes: Task 2 involved re-diagnosing the same 100 images with support from the 6 closest images retrieved by SE-CBIR. Diagnosis accuracy increased by 22.7 points (from 62.2% to 84.9%). Confidence increased by 24% (from 3.11 to 3.86). Melanoma overdiagnosis (nonmelanoma misclassified as melanoma) decreased from 171 cases in Task 1 to 81 cases in Task 2. The SE-CBIR majority-voting prediction (89%) was outperformed by the 2 best-performing human participants when provided with retrieved images.

architecture_used.input_size: 380x380 pixels (for standard CBIR); 4-channel input (3 RGB channels + 1 saliency map channel) for SE-CBIR fine-tuning

architecture_details.modification: Top layer removed and replaced with average pooling, batch normalization, and 2 combinations of dropout and dense layers.
architecture_details.feature_extraction: Deep features extracted just after the last convolutional layer and before the classification layers.
architecture_details.saliency_integration: Vanilla gradient saliency maps computed from the initial classifier. For SE-CBIR fine-tuning, original image and saliency map concatenated to form a 4-channel input.
architecture_details.similarity_metric: Cosine similarity for ranking retrieved images

training_details.optimizer: Not explicitly named, but adaptive learning rate approach used
training_details.learning_rate_schedule: Initial ramp-up followed by exponential decay
training_details.data_augmentation: Geometric augmentations, noise, distortion, brightness/contrast, color modifications (on the fly); random flips and rotations during SE-CBIR fine-tuning
training_details.data_split: 80:10:10 stratified split for training, validation, and testing

preprocessing_techniques: Resizing images to 380x380 pixels to match EfficientNetB4 input resolution
Concatenation of original dermoscopic images with vanilla gradient saliency maps to create 4-channel inputs (3 RGB channels + 1 saliency map channel) for SE-CBIR fine-tuning
Removal of follow-up images from the HAM10000 dataset to maximize diversity
Data augmentation applied on the fly during training: geometric augmentations, noise, distortion, brightness/contrast modifications, and color modifications
Random flips and rotations applied during SE-CBIR fine-tuning stage
Coarse dropout applied to enhance regularization

data_augmentation_techniques.details: Applied on the fly during training using albumentations libraries to avoid overfitting.
Applied on the fly during training using albumentations libraries.
Applied on the fly during training using albumentations libraries.
Applied on the fly during training using albumentations libraries.
Images were randomly cropped to the expected EfficientNetB4 input resolution (380x380 pixels).
Applied during the SE-CBIR fine-tuning stage; no other data augmentation was performed during this specific stage.

conclusions: Saliency-Enhanced Content-Based Image Retrieval (SE-CBIR) improves retrieval accuracy by 7% (from 77% to 84%) compared to traditional CNN-based CBIR approaches.
The use of SE-CBIR as a diagnostic support tool increased dermatologists' and residents' diagnosis accuracy by 22% (from 62% to 84%).
SE-CBIR support increased the overall confidence in lesion diagnosis by 24% (from 3.11 to 3.86 on a 5-point Likert scale).
The implementation of SE-CBIR reduced melanoma overdiagnosis (misclassifying nonmelanoma lesions as melanoma) by 53%.
Inter-rater agreement among participants improved from fair (Cohen κ=0.35) in the baseline task to substantial (Cohen κ=0.66) when using SE-CBIR support.
The SE-CBIR algorithm's majority-voting prediction accuracy (89%) was outperformed by the two best-performing human participants when they were provided with retrieved images.
Interpretability via saliency maps is a key factor for increasing trust and acceptance of AI tools in routine dermatological consultation.

The study acknowledges that the HAM10000 dataset is composed of hand-picked lesions, which may present diagnostic challenges without patient context, and that underrepresented classes (such as akiec) may require larger statistics for significant improvement.
important_remarks: The study relies on a single dataset (HAM10000), which is highly unbalanced (67% melanocytic nevi) and composed of hand-picked lesions, limiting generalizability to underrepresented classes like actinic keratosis (akiec).
The reader study participants lacked patient context, which is a known limitation that can penalize diagnosis accuracy in difficult cases.
Saliency maps are noted as fragile; different calculation methods may yield different results, potentially affecting interpretability consistency.
The study did not include external validation on independent datasets or diverse user groups (e.g., general practitioners, nurses), restricting the assessment of real-world clinical utility.
The AI model's majority-voting prediction (89% accuracy) was outperformed by the two best-performing human participants when they were provided with retrieved images.
No formal hypothesis testing (e.g., p-values) was reported; results are presented as descriptive statistics and Cohen's kappa coefficients.
The study acknowledges that data augmentation cannot resolve class imbalance issues for rare classes like akiec, necessitating additional data collection.
